original_abstract:
Copper-based catalysts are frontline candidates for converting CO2 into methane via electrolysis, yet achieving high selectivity remains a formidable challenge due to the complex, multi-step nature of the reaction. In this work, we propose a dual-level design strategy that optimizes both the atomic structure of the active sites and the mesoscale architecture of the carbon support to steer the reaction pathway toward methane.

By utilizing the microphase separation of a PAN-b-PMMA block copolymer, we synthesized a carbon fiber support characterized by an interconnected network of uniform mesochannels. These bicontinuous channels create a unique local microenvironment: they are inherently water-deficient and CO2-rich, which effectively suppresses the competing hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). Furthermore, the long-range confinement of these pores increases the residence time of CO intermediates, facilitating their deep reduction to methane.

Crucially, operando investigations using in situ X-ray absorption and Raman spectroscopy reveal a dynamic structural evolution under reaction conditions. We found that the initial atomically dispersed Cu-N3 sites undergo a reversible reconstruction into nitrogen- and hydroxyl-stabilized Cu3 clusters (N,OH-Cu3). Theoretical calculations confirm that these newly identified N,OH-Cu3 clusters possess a moderate affinity for *CO and significantly lower the energy barrier for *CO hydrogenation, while simultaneously hindering the C–C coupling pathway that leads to multicarbon products.

As a result of this synergistic multiscale engineering, the catalyst achieves a remarkable methane Faradaic efficiency of 74.2% at an industry-relevant current density of 300 mA cm−2. This research provides a powerful framework for navigating complex interfacial catalytic pathways by integrating atomic-level site design with mesoscale transport control.
humanized_abstract:
Copper-based catalysts are one of the most widely studied candidates for the CO2 reduction to CH4 via electrolysis. However, a high selectivity towards CH4 is not yet reached, mainly because of the immense complexity of the electrochemical process which typically involves numerous steps, which often leads to undesired side-products. Herein, we demonstrate that through a clever dual-level design, in which the atomic configuration at the active sites as well as the mesostructure of the carbon support are tailored, the formation of CH4 can be highly promoted.

A carbon fiber support was prepared by making full use of the microphase separation of PAN-b-PMMA block copolymer. The obtained carbon fiber support has an interconnected network of uniform mesochannels. The bicontinuous structure leads to the formation of a local microenvironment that is water deficient and CO2 abundant, thus the HER reaction can be greatly suppressed. Meanwhile, the long-range pore confinement can prolong the residence time of CO species and promote their deep reduction into CH4.

Operando studies using in situ X-ray absorption and Raman spectroscopy demonstrate that the structure of such single-atom catalysts is not fixed and evolves under operating conditions. The initial atomically dispersed Cu–N3 sites undergo a reversible structural transformation into nitrogen and hydroxyl-stabilized Cu3 clusters (N,OH-Cu3). Modeling studies on the identified N,OH-Cu3 cluster species demonstrate that the Cu3 clusters have only a moderate affinity to *CO and that they have lower energy barriers for *CO hydrogenation while having higher activation energies for C–C bond coupling leading to multi-carbon molecules.

Multi-Scale Synergistic Engineering of Solid Oxide Electrolyzer Catalysts for Enhanced Performance through Mesoscale Transport Control and Atomic-Scale Site Design Integration at the Catalytic Interface is published in the journal Applied Catalysis B: Environmental. The catalyst realizes a methane Faradaic efficiency of 74.2% at a current density of 300 mA cm−2, a value that is relevant to SOEC operation. This work presents a new route for navigating the intricacies of catalytic interfaces while bridging the length scales of site design and catalytic performance.
